- Only attack-designated units that participated in the GA may advance (13.12.1).
- Advance is **never mandatory** — you can always stop or decline.
- After completing the advance, remove all attack designations from participants.

### Maximum advance distance (13.12.3)

Count the defender's hex as the 1st hex.

- Units in **Tac or Exploit**: **1 hex** (into defender's hex only)
- **Leg in PA**: **2 hexes**
- **Mech in PA**: **4 hexes**

If the defender was eliminated before retreating, advance is capped at the total unresolved hits (mandatory + discretionary) (13.12.3).

### Path restrictions (13.12.4, 13.12.5)

- Normal path: follow the defender's retreat path.
- Mech units may deviate if every hex entered is clear terrain or road-connected.
- Mech must stop in the first off-road hex containing woods / forest / marsh / bocage / hedgerow / soft ground / swamp / RR embankment / constricted terrain.
- Must stop if advancing into a hex **adjacent to an ET, Fort, or intact Ft Area** — even if empty (13.12.5a).
- Mud caps advance at 1 hex (Ldr can +1 to 2 max) (13.12.5b).
- Night/ENA caps advance at 1 hex (Ldr +1 to 2) (13.12.5c).
- Low-fuel Mech can advance only 1 hex; no-fuel Mech cannot advance at all (13.12.5d).

Mech Recon in PA can advance one hex farther than the retreat path allows if the whole advance is on roads or clear terrain (13.12.3) — the deepest penetration tool in the game. Park Recon in assault stacks when pursuit matters.

Standoff units **cannot advance** (13.5.2). If you used standoff to cross a river or bypass combined-arms rules, those units stay put no matter how far the defender ran. Factor this into your post-combat position plan before committing to standoff.

### End of segment

Once all GAs in the segment are complete, remove **all AS markers** from both sides (13.13.0). AS never carries over past the Combat Phase.

chapters.title: Active Player Exploit Phase
chapters.intro: Your second bite at the apple. Units already in Exploit mode move and can overrun enemy units on the fly. The Active Exploit Phase runs identically to the Inactive one (3.3.2f) — same procedure, different clock.

decisions.title: Which Units Can Exploit This Phase
decisions.when: Start of the Active Exploit Phase (3.3.2f). You only move units that are already in Exploit mode (5.3.0).

blocks.text: Exploit mode is set earlier — during **Mode Determination** (5.3.0) or by releasing an MR formation into Exploit at the start of a friendly Exploit Phase (5.4.3). In this phase you just *use* what you prepared.

### Who moves

1) Units with an Exploit marker placed during Mode Determination (5.3.0).
2) Individual Mech Recon units that entered Exploit alone (5.3.1).
3) A formation or Rgt just released from MR into Exploit (5.4.3). This is your only mid-turn way to activate new exploit units.

**Who does NOT move:** units in Tac, PA, MR, or Strat; any unit OoS (must exit Exploit in the Admin Phase per 5.3.2); fatigued units (they can't be in Exploit at all per 5.3.0); units that lost their Exploit marker earlier this GT (5.3.2).
If you didn't place Exploit markers in Mode Determination, your only option now is to release an MR formation into Exploit (5.4.3) — and only Mech formations or single Mech Recon units qualify.

decisions.title: How Exploit Movement Differs
decisions.when: Moving Exploit units during the Active Exploit Phase (5.3.4). Different rules than the Movement Phase.

blocks.text:
### Key differences from the Movement Phase

1) **Only half your MA in AM/PM** (round up) (5.3.4). You get a *second* half-MA move in this phase after the Inactive Exploit Phase moved its half. Your total is still one full MA across both phases.
2) **Night/ENA: full MA, but pick one phase** — Inactive or Active, not both (5.3.4, 3.3.5). Choose based on where the tactical window opens.
3) **Move in order** (5.3.4): Mech road movement first, then non-road. Same order as Tac movement in the Movement Phase (7.5.2).
4) **No additional MP cost for moving adjacent to enemies** if the destination is MCT (5.3.4a). This is the signature exploit advantage.
5) **GS attacks are legal against moving units** (5.3.4c). Each enemy hex entered within 6 hexes, not in MCT, observable = one GS attack possible.
**OhS units die fast in Exploit.** An OhS unit that spends more than 3 MPs in an Exploit Phase is immediately marked OoS (15.5.2). Check supply status before committing deep moves.

If an Exploit unit starts the phase adjacent to an enemy unit in **PA mode**, it *must* retreat up to its max retreat distance and then reverts to Tac mode — Exploit marker removed (5.3.4a). Adjacent enemies in TA mode don't trigger this.

decisions.title: Overruns: Attacking Without Stopping
decisions.when: During Exploit movement, when a Mech unit enters or begins adjacent to an enemy hex (5.3.4b, 5.3.5).

blocks.text: An overrun is the whole point of Exploit. One Mech unit (or one stack using special stacking) combines Exploit movement and GA in a single action, potentially chaining multiple overruns in one phase.

### Steps

1) Declare the overrun when entering (or starting adjacent to) the target hex (5.3.4b).
2) Pay +1 MP on top of normal entry costs including adjacency costs if applicable (5.3.4b).
3) Defender may conduct a **medium** Art FS mission if the target hex is ET/Fort/Fortified area; otherwise, if the defender is a Bn., a **light** Art FS (5.3.5).
4) Resolve GA with no combat reserves on either side (5.3.5). All other shifts/DRMs apply.
5) **Declare:** will you convert all discretionary hits to step losses? If no, the overrun is unsuccessful (5.3.5b).
6) If yes, compare attacker total hits vs defender total hits. Attacker < defender = successful overrun.
7) **Success (5.3.5a):** attacker inflicts all hits as mandatory step losses (no max step loss). Defender resolves normally. You may continue moving with remaining MP, ignoring overrun units.
8) **Failure (5.3.5b):** attacker returns to the hex it came from. Remove the Exploit marker. Apply all hits as if attacker chose retreat option.

### Who can't overrun

- OoS units, OoC units, fatigued units.
- Leg class units transported by TP/Allied inherent transport (Kangaroos are the exception, 4.6.5).
- Units entering through a non-MCT hex adjacent to more than one enemy-occupied hex (5.3.4b). At night, treat all OCT as MCT.

The 'convert all discretionary hits to step losses' gate (5.3.5) forces you to commit: an overrun is only 'successful' if you deal real damage. You can choose to take your numerical hits as a retreat result normally (keeping the discretionary hits as fatigue), but then the overrun fails and you lose Exploit mode.

items: Is the target in ET/Fort/Fortified area? -> Expect a medium defensive Art mission before you roll.
Do I have MP to spare after the overrun? -> If yes, I can chain into another hex on success.
Am I OhS and spending >3 MP? -> I'm OoS immediately; check if the overrun is still worth it.
Is the hex I'm passing through non-MCT with multiple adjacent enemies? -> Can't overrun from there.

blocks.text: Full overrun procedure and defender FS rules live in 5.3.5 — worth a read before your first overrun.

chapters.title: Air Resupply Segment
chapters.intro: Last-ditch supply for isolated pockets and depleted OhS HQs. Only runs in AM/PM GTs (3.3.2g) and only in clear or POvr weather (20.5). ATP are scarce — scenario rules set the pool.

decisions.title: Where to Drop and What It Buys You
decisions.when: Air Resupply Segment of the Admin Phase (3.3.2g), AM or PM only, clear or POvr.

blocks.text:
### Steps

1) Declare ATP for the mission (20.5.1). Do **not** adjust ATP count for atmospheric conditions.
2) Declare escorting ASup AP, then the inactive player declares intercepting AP. Resolve ASup (20.5.1, 20.6.2).
3) Declare the DZ hex — clear, hedgerow, or rough terrain; may contain village/location; not enemy occupied or enemy adjacent (20.5.2).
4) Roll 1d10 per ATP. DRMs: **+1** per 4 enemy units within 3 hexes (Flak-capable units within range count double), **+1** if POvr (20.5.3).
5) Modified DR of 8 or less = 1 air supply point delivered. Unmodified 0 always succeeds.

### What 1 air supply point buys (15.5.1b)

- **1 OhS point** to an army HQ, **or**
- **2 OhS points** to a corps HQ, **or**
- **4 OhS points** to a Div/BG HQ.
- If no HQ can receive (must trace GenS of 3 hexes or less to DZ), 1 point **removes 10 OoS markers** from non-HQ units within 3 hexes of the DZ (not from HQs).
Smaller HQs = more OhS per point. Resupplying a division gives 4x the effect of supplying the army HQ — so drop at the lowest-level HQ that actually needs it.
The DZ hex can't be adjacent to an enemy unit (20.5.2). That usually means you're dropping outside your pocket — the receiving HQ still needs a GenS path of 3 hexes or less to the DZ (15.5.1b).

decisions.title: Escorts and Interception
decisions.when: During Step 2 of the air supply mission (20.5.1, 20.6).

blocks.text: Air supply missions can be intercepted like any non-GI air mission (20.6.1). You may escort with ASup AP; the opposing side may intercept with ASup AP.

### Outcome math (20.6.2)

1) Each escort AP aborts one intercept AP and vice versa (mutual kill).
2) Each surviving intercept AP aborts one ATP (not one mission — one ATP).
3) Aborted ATP do not deliver and are not available elsewhere this GD.
If the enemy has ASup AP available, budget at least one escort per ATP. A single uncontested intercept can abort a whole ATP.

chapters.title: Supply Determination Segment
chapters.intro: The consequential housekeeping step. You trace GenS from PSS through army -> corps -> formation -> unit, mark failures OhS or OoS, and check isolation. Get this wrong and your attack plan for next turn silently falls apart.

decisions.title: Tracing Supply in the Right Order
decisions.when: Supply Determination Segment (15.1.1). HQs first, then units.

blocks.text:
### Steps

1) Trace HQ paths first (15.1.1). PSS -> army HQ -> corps HQ -> formation HQ. Each segment is an HQ-to-HQ path: Mech class, max 18 Mech MP, clear terrain or primary/secondary roads only (15.2.2).
2) Mark any HQ that fails as OhS and immediately roll for OhS points (15.5.1).
3) Then trace each non-HQ unit from its formation HQ (15.2.3):
   - **Leg formations:** 12 Leg MP.
   - **Axis Mech formations:** 12 Mech MP.
   - **Allied Mech formations:** 18 Mech MP.
4) Mark failures per 15.1.2 (OhS, OoS, isolated).
5) An OhS HQ only traces half its normal distance to subordinates (15.2.5).
**Don't skip HQ tracing first** (15.1.1). If a corps HQ goes OhS, its whole supply chain shortens for this segment and you may need to accept extended paths or OhS rolls for subordinate formations.

Axis Mech formations get only 12 Mech MP of reach (vs Allied 18) — a deliberate asymmetry that makes deep Axis penetrations fragile. Plan Axis advance depth against supply reach, not just enemy positions.

### Weather hits path length (15.2.6)

- **Mud:** -3 MP unless tracing exclusively on primary/secondary roads.
- **Snow:** -1 MP unless on primary/secondary roads.

Apply weather *before* halving for OhS or extending for extended GenS.

decisions.title: OhS vs OoS vs Isolated — Knowing the Difference
decisions.when: Marking units after trace fails (15.1.2).

blocks.text: There are five supply states. Get the marker right or you'll play the wrong penalty table.

### The five states (15.1.2)

1) **In GenS:** full function. No marker.
2) **OhS:** trace failed, but has on-hand supply. Functions normally until OhS runs out (15.5.0).
3) **OhS & Isolated:** OhS + no GenS path of any length + no friendly GenS unit within 3 hexes.
4) **OoS:** trace failed and OhS exhausted. Severe penalties kick in (15.6.0).
5) **OoS & Isolated:** OoS + no GenS path + no friendly GenS within 3 hexes. Surrender check each Command Phase (15.7.1).

### When an OhS unit becomes OoS (15.5.2)

A unit marked OhS stays OhS until it does any of:

1) Expends >1/2 of its printed MA.
2) Conducts an offensive GA.
3) Defends using >1/2 its defensive CS.
4) Participates in an Art FS mission.
5) Expends >3 MPs in Exploit mode (overrunning with <=3 MP and <=1/2 attack CS is the one exception).

Immediately flip the marker from OhS to OoS when any trigger fires.

OoS effects are brutal (15.6.0): half MA, half CS, can't enter PA/Exploit/Strat/CR, can't replace, Art units auto-mark AD and can't replenish. But **Arm/AT factors are NOT halved** — armor still matters in defense.
Units eliminated while isolated **don't generate recycle steps** and cost extra ReP to resurrect (22.2.0, 22.5.2a). Preventing isolation is always cheaper than replacing the losses.

decisions.title: Rolling OhS for Stranded HQs
decisions.when: Immediately when an HQ fails its trace (15.5.1).

blocks.text:
### Steps (15.5.1)

1) Roll 1d10 with cumulative DRMs:
   - **-4** formation HQ, **-3** corps HQ.
   - **-1** if no GenS path of any length exists.
   - **+1** if in a town/city hex.
   - **+2** if US HQ.
2) Read the result:
   - **3 or less:** 4 OhS points.
   - **4-6:** 6 OhS points.
   - **7 or more:** 8 OhS points.
3) Record the number on the HQ (pocket supply marker + record track).

### Spending OhS (15.5.1a)

- 1 OhS point = GenS for the whole chain below this HQ until the next Admin Phase.
- Convert OhS -> FP: 1:1.
- Convert OhS -> AmP: army HQ 1:2, corps HQ 1:1, formation HQ cannot convert.
- Remaining OhS after conversion = the HQ's ADV (subordinate Art uses this).
- **Start of next Admin Phase with 0 OhS = immediate OoS.**
US formation HQs in a town are the best OhS rollers: -4 (formation) +1 (town) +2 (US) = net -1. That usually lands 6 or 8 points, sometimes both.

chapters.title: Ammo Replenishment Segment
chapters.intro: This is not the logistics-phase AmP delivery (16.3.1) — it's the narrow end-of-turn step where you un-AD your depleted Art units (16.3.7). Both sides act in a fixed three-step order.

decisions.title: Delivery vs Replenishment — Don't Confuse Them
decisions.when: Ammo Replenishment Segment of the Admin Phase (16.3.7).

blocks.text:
**Ammo Delivery (16.3.1)** happens once per GD in the Joint Logistics Phase — it sets army/corps ADV and stockpiles AmP.

**Ammo Replenishment (16.3.7)** happens every Admin Phase — it removes AD markers from depleted Art units so they can fire again.

### The three steps (phase description in 3.3.2g, procedure in 16.3.7)

1) **Active player** expends AmP to replenish AD Art (16.3.7a).
2) **Active player** rolls 1d10 per remaining AD Art unit to replenish by DR (16.3.7b).
3) **Inactive player** expends AmP to replenish AD Art (16.3.7a).

Note: the inactive player can *only* spend AmP during enemy Admin Phases — no DR attempts until their own (16.3.7).

### AmP replenishment math (16.3.7a)

- **3 AD Art per 1 AmP spent.** Replenishing fewer than 3 still costs a full AmP — waste is lost.
- Active player *must* spend AmP first, then roll (16.3.7a).
- Unsupported Art can *only* replenish by AmP (15.4.3).

An Art unit marked OhS that **participated in an FS mission** is immediately flipped to OoS and can only replenish via an OhS HQ with remaining OhS (16.3.7). Check which Art fired while on OhS before starting this step.

decisions.title: DR Replenishment — Know Your ADV
decisions.when: Step 2, active player only (16.3.7b).

blocks.text:
### Steps (16.3.7b)

1) Roll 1d10 per AD Art unit.
2) **+2 DRM** if the Art unit is using extended GenS (15.2.4).
3) Modified DR <= superior HQ ADV = remove AD.
4) Modified DR > ADV = still AD.
5) One attempt per unit per friendly Admin Phase.
Keep Art units within normal (not extended) GenS of their supported HQ to avoid the +2 DR penalty. Extended GenS also blocks intensive fire entirely (15.2.4).
Unsupported Art (corps/formation that you chose not to support in 16.3.2 step 4/7) **cannot roll** — only AmP replenishment works (15.4.3). Same for Art with ADV zero.

chapters.title: Fatigue Recovery Segment
chapters.intro: Quiet but vital. Units that sat still this GT can shed one fatigue level (14.3.0). Fatigue 2 units take two such turns to recover — plan rotations early.

decisions.title: Who Qualifies to Shed a Fatigue Level
decisions.when: Fatigue Recovery Segment (14.3.0).

blocks.text:
A unit removes **one** fatigue level (max) if all apply (14.3.0):

1) Did not move in the preceding friendly Movement Phase.
2) Did not participate in a GA **and** received no FS mission result in the preceding friendly Combat Phase.
3) **Mech unit:** not adjacent to an enemy **and** not observed by an enemy ground unit.
4) **Leg unit:** not observed by an enemy ground unit (dismounted units count as Leg here).

### Why the distinction matters

- Fatigue 1 halves MA, CS, and barrage strength (14.2.1).
- Fatigue 2 quarters MA and defensive CS, blocks PA mode and attack designation, and bars FS participation (14.2.2).
- F2 -> F1 -> fresh takes **two** consecutive qualifying GTs.

During a **lull** (24.0), all units on both sides recover fully at the GT the lull ends — *unless* units of their formation participated in a GA the previous GT.
Leaving a fatigued Mech unit adjacent to or observed by enemies blocks recovery. Pull fatigued armor one hex back behind LOS-blocking terrain if you want it ready to fight.
Units in an **MR bonus period** don't take fatigue during ENA or rest GTs (5.4.4d). That's one of the main reasons MR is worth the 9-GT setup cost for sustained operations.

chapters.title: Delay Marker Removal Segment
chapters.intro: Both sides can clean up delay markers here (7.13.3). The owner clears any of theirs; the non-owner clears enemy delay markers in hexes their units occupy.

decisions.title: Remove Yours or Leave Them?
decisions.when: Delay Marker Removal Segment of the Admin Phase (7.13.3).

blocks.text:
### Who can remove what (7.13.3)

1) **Owner:** may remove any friendly delay markers, no restrictions.
2) **Non-owner:** may remove enemy delay markers **only** in hexes where a friendly unit occupies the delay marker hex.

Delay markers left in place generate an immediate MH on enemies entering the hex (7.13.2). Friendly units can move through them freely, but the marker is removed if a friendly unit ends any phase there.

### Why leave them

- They still MH enemy movement next turn.
- They still force an offensive PRC on enemy attack-designated units entering the hex during the Attack Designation Segment (7.13.2).

### Why clear them

- A delay marker in a hex you plan to attack *from* next turn will force a PRC on your own attack designation (7.13.2). Clear before your unit enters the hex to stack.
- You can't reuse markers removed in the same Movement Phase (7.13.0), so don't churn them mid-GT.
Delay markers are scarce (game supplies set the cap per 7.13.0). Keep the ones that block enemy approaches, clear the ones in your own attack staging hexes.

chapters.title: Update Game Turn Indicator
chapters.intro: Top-level phase — not part of either player turn. Check end-of-game, then advance the GT marker (3.3.4). Most things reset with the new GT; a few carry over.

decisions.title: What the GT Advance Actually Changes
decisions.when: End of the Axis Player Turn, before a new GT starts (3.3.4).

blocks.text:
### Steps (3.3.4)

1) If the final scenario GT just completed **or** either side met a sudden death victory condition, the game is over — consult scenario victory conditions.
2) Otherwise, advance the GT marker on the GTRT to the next box.
3) Start the new GT at the Joint Phase (3.3.1) unless a non-AM GT — then at the Allied Mode Determination Phase (3.3.2).

### What resets with the new GT

- Active/Inactive designation cycles back to the Joint Phase.
- Air Allocation, GI, and other per-GT air actions re-roll in the new GT's Joint Phase (3.3.1a).
- PA/TA attack designation must be re-evaluated each GT (12.1.0 via Attacker Status Adjustment, Combat Phase).
- AS markers were already cleared at the end of each Combat Phase (13.13.0).

### What carries across the GT boundary

- **Mode markers** (Exploit, MR, PA, Strat, CR, Tac) persist until voluntarily changed in Mode Determination or involuntarily removed (5.0).
- **Supply markers** (OhS, OoS, isolated) persist until the next Supply Determination Segment trace confirms or clears them.
- **Fatigue markers** persist until a Fatigue Recovery Segment removes a level.
- **Delay markers** persist until removed per 7.13.3.
- **Army stockpiles** of AmP and FP persist across GTs up to the cap (20 without a depot, 40 with one; 16.3.6, 16.4.1).

The Joint Logistics Phase only runs on **AM GTs** (3.3.1). ADV and AmP/FP allocations you set in the AM carry through PM and Night of the same GD — you don't get a second chance until tomorrow morning.
Time of day (AM/PM/Night) and ENA markers are all tracked on the GTRT alongside the GT number. The physical board shows this — the app just mirrors the time-of-day toggle.
Night GTs use the same SoP as AM/PM, but each side may conduct Exploit activity in only one of its two Exploit Phases (3.3.5). ENA is optional and adds a special SoP on top of the night GT (3.4).

chapters.title: Pre-Game Day — Overview
chapters.intro: Before either side takes a turn, both players jointly set up the Game Day: air, weather, command, logistics, and fuel. Decisions made here lock in for the entire GD — you cannot reshuffle AP missions or TP allocations mid-day. Treat this as mission planning, not bookkeeping.

decisions.title: What locks for the Game Day vs. what varies each GT
decisions.when: Before the AM Allied Player Turn begins (3.3.1).

blocks.text:
### Phases run in order

1) Air Allocation (3.3.1a, AM only).
2) Weather (3.3.1b, every GT).
3) Command (3.3.1c, AM only).
4) Logistics (3.3.1d, AM only).
5) Fuel Value Determination (3.3.1e, every GT).

**Weather and Fuel repeat every GT; everything else is set once for the GD.** Atmospheric conditions change AM vs. PM vs. Night, so the GI roll and fuel value roll happen each GT. Your AP mission allocations, TP assignments, attachments, and rest-GT pick do not.

items: Do I know the weather forecast? Check before committing AP — you're allocating now but may not know conditions yet (3.3.1a precedes 3.3.1b).
Which corps absolutely must attack this GD? Plan TP, AP, and ADV around them.
Which formations are resting this GD? Don't waste supply/ammo on them.
Am I over 3 corps on any army? If so, the ADV cascade penalty kicks in — consider detaching during Command Phase (9.6.1).

decisions.title: Plan weather-first
decisions.when: When drafting your AP and logistics decisions (19.3.0).

blocks.text: Weather cascades into almost everything pre-game day decisions touch: AP values are halved in POvr and quartered in Ovr; Storm or Snow cancels air operations entirely (19.3.0). Supply interdiction and ground interdiction both pull from the AP pool, so a bad weather forecast means fewer effective points everywhere.
You allocate AP **before** weather is rolled (3.3.1a precedes 3.3.1b). With variable weather, you're committing blind — plan for the worst plausible condition on scenarios that roll weather.

chapters.title: Player Turn — Overview
chapters.intro: The active player runs the full sequence: mode determination, construction, movement, combat, exploit, then end-of-turn housekeeping. The inactive player has one window during your turn — the Inactive Exploit Phase — so plan defensively for that. Axis and Allied turns use the same sequence (3.3.3).

decisions.title: Plan the turn before committing to any mode
decisions.when: Start of your active player turn (3.3.2 / 3.3.3).

blocks.text:
### Phases in order

1) Mode Determination (sets what each formation can do this turn).
2) Construction (bridges, fieldworks, demolitions).
3) Combat Reserve Designation.
4) Movement.
5) Quick Construction.
6) Enemy Inactive Exploit (the other side moves now).
7) Attacker Designation → Replacements → Fire Support → Ground Assault.
8) Active Exploit.
9) Air Resupply → Supply Determination → Ammo Replenishment → Fatigue Recovery → Delay Marker Removal.

**Mode choice cascades through the whole turn and cannot be undone.** A formation in Strat mode cannot attack this turn; one in Tac mode has reduced MA. Figure out which formations attack, which exploit, and which rest **before** you pick modes (5.0).

Use the **inactive** half of each GT — when the other player is active — to observe enemy dispositions, spot supply weaknesses, and plan your own turn. By the time your active phases begin, the decisions should already be made.

items: What's the weather this GT? Bad weather means consolidate, not attack (19.0).
Which formations are attacking? They need TA mode, ammo, and fire support.
Which formations are exploiting? They need Exploit-eligible mode and fresh MA.
Which formations are resting this GD? Keep them off the front line (3.5.3).

decisions.title: Anticipate the enemy's Inactive Exploit window
decisions.when: When positioning units during your Movement Phase (3.3.2d / 3.3.3d).

blocks.text: After your Movement Phase but before your Ground Assault, the other side gets an Inactive Exploit Phase — their Exploit-eligible formations can move and overrun. This is your biggest reactive risk: units left in the open after your Movement can be cut off or engaged before you've even fought your combats.

Do **not** leave a salient or exposed flank after your Movement Phase. A lone unit in open terrain is easy prey for the enemy's Exploit: without adjacent friendlies there's no MP surcharge (7.8.0) slowing them down, and unless you have red-hex Art or heavy AT/Flak in range to trigger a Movement Halt (7.7.1, 7.7.2), an Exploit-mode formation can walk past and isolate it from its GenS trace (7.7.5).
